title: Charged Abelian Higgs phase transitions in three-dimensional compact lattice U(1) gauge models with multicharge scalar matter
authors: Claudio Bonati, Filippo Mariani, Ettore Vicari
abstract: We consider three-dimensional (3D) lattice Abelian Higgs models, with compact U(1) gauge variables coupled to a doubly-charged $N$-component complex scalar field (CLAH). We focus on their phase transitions between the disordered-confined (DC) and ordered-deconfined (OD) phases. When they are continuous they belong to the 3D Abelian Higgs (AH) universality class associated with the stable charged fixed point (CFP) of the renormalization-group flow of the 3D AH field theory, or scalar electrodynamics, describing $N$-component complex scalar fields minimally coupled to a U(1) gauge field. This CFP exists only for a sufficiently large number of components, i.e., $N \ge N_d^*$, where the integer $N_d^*$ depends on the spatial dimension $d$ (for example $N_4^*=183$). To estimate $N_3^*$, we look for the minimum number $N_{\rm cL}$ of scalar components of 3D doubly-charged CLAH models developing continuous transitions along their DC-OD transition line. For this purpose, we present finite-size scaling analyses of Monte Carlo simulations for $N\in[4,10]$, up to lattice sizes $L\approx 100$. The results provide evidence of continuous DC-OD transitions for $N=10$, and weak first-order transitions for $N\le 7$. They are not conclusive for $N=8,\,9$. Therefore, we estimate $N_{\rm cL}=9(1)$.
summary: This work analyzes phase transitions in 3D lattice Abelian Higgs models with charged scalar matter. Using Monte Carlo simulations and finite-size scaling, the authors test how the transition nature changes with the number of scalar components ($N$). They estimate that the critical number of components required for a continuous transition is $N_{cL}=9$.
main_problem: The paper investigates the nature of phase transitions (specifically the Disordered-Confined (DC) to Ordered-Deconfined (OD) transition) in three-dimensional lattice Abelian Higgs models coupled to multicharge scalar matter.
main_result: The analysis provides evidence that the minimum number of scalar components ($N_{cL}$) required for a continuous transition is 9, leading to an estimate for the critical component number $N_*^3 = 9$.
method: The primary method involves performing finite-size scaling (FSS) analyses on Monte Carlo simulations to distinguish between continuous and first-order phase transitions.
model_or_system: The system is a 3D lattice Abelian Higgs (AH) model, specifically the Charged Lattice Abelian Higgs (CLAH) model, defined by a Hamiltonian involving U(1) gauge fields and an $N$-component complex scalar field with a doubly-positive charge ($q=2$).
key_observables: The analysis uses the Binder parameter ($U$), the ratio of correlation lengths ($R_\xi$), and the susceptibility ($\chi_Q$) to diagnose the order of the transition.
important_parameters: Key parameters include the number of scalar components ($N$), the charge ($q=2$), and the lattice size ($L$), with the focus on identifying the critical value $N_{cL}$.
main_assumptions: The authors assume that the minimum number of components exhibiting continuous transitions ($N_{cL}$) directly estimates the critical value $N_*^3$ relevant to the continuum field theory.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the scaling behavior of the Binder cumulant ($U$) and the correlation length ratio ($R_\xi$) for different $N$, showing clear scaling for $N=10$ (continuous) and deviations suggesting weak first-order behavior for $N \le 7$.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the physical model and the theoretical context (AH universality class), details the numerical methods (MC simulations and FSS), presents results for various $N$ values, and concludes by estimating the critical component number $N_*^3$.
why_interesting: The study of lattice gauge theories and critical phenomena is foundational to understanding emergent order in many-body quantum systems, providing theoretical tools applicable to strongly correlated materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Sketch of the phase diagram of the 3D CLAH models for generic integer charge q > 1 and N > 1. It presents three phases: the disordered-confined (DC), the ordered-deconfined (OD), and the ordered-confined (OC) phase. They are sep- arated by different DC-OD, DC-OC and OC-CD transition lines [4]. We also mention that CLAH models become equiv- alent to the CPN−1 model for κ = 0, to the O(2N) vec- tor model for κ →∞, and to the lattice Zq gauge model for J →∞. Continuous transitions belonging to the 3D N- component AH universality classes develop along the DC-OD line, for sufficiently large N. The line κ = 1 is expected to cross the DC-OD line in doubly-charged (q = 2) CLAH mod- els for...
FIG. 2: The ratio Rξ for the CLAH with N = 10 close to the transition point Jc at κ = 1, where the data sets for different values of L cross.

FIG. 3: Estimates of the critical exponent ν for N = 10, extracted from the behavior of Rξ close to Jc at κ = 1.To allow for the leading O(L−ω) scaling corrections, we used the values ω = 0.6, 0.8, 1.0, and perfomed fits of the data for lattice sizes L ≥Lmin. The horizontal band denotes the estimate of ν obtained for the NCLAH model in [84].
FIG. 4: Scaling of the cumulant U for the CLAH with N = 10 and κ = 1, using ν = 0.64(2) and Jc = 0.31870(2).

FIG. 5: The Binder cumulant U vs the ratio Rξ for the CLAH with N = 10 close to transition point Jc at κ = 1. The scaling behavior is reasonable. We observe larger scaling corrections around the maximum values of U, which should get asymp- totically suppressed as L−ω with ω ≈0.8.
